definition: A slow-cooked aromatic base of finely chopped onion, carrot, and celery sweated in olive oil until soft. Italian cooks build many sauces and braises on top of this gentle starting layer.

substitutions.note: milder, sweeter base; hold the salt back a touch
adds a mild anise note that suits seafood

origin: Northern Italian home-cooking; the foundation step in many regional ragùs.

definition: Whole spices briefly bloomed in hot oil or ghee, then poured over a finished dish to lift its aroma. Mustard seeds, cumin, dried chilies, and curry leaves are the usual suspects.

substitutions.note: vegan-friendly; loses some richness, gains heat tolerance
fresh has substantially more aroma; reduce quantity if dried

origin: Traditional finishing technique across the subcontinent.

definition: The savory taste sensation produced by glutamates and certain nucleotides. It registers as a deep, mouth-filling savoriness distinct from sweet, sour, salty, and bitter.

substitutions.note: vegetarian umami source; expect a less funky depth
vegan dashi alternative; longer steep needed

origin: Identified and named by Kikunae Ikeda in 1908.

definition: A warming blend of ground spices typically including cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, cumin, coriander, and black pepper. Added near the end of cooking to preserve its top-note aromatics.

substitutions.note: fresher, brighter aroma; the easiest 10-minute upgrade
different profile; works in a pinch but lacks the warm finishing notes

origin: North Indian household spice blend; recipe varies family to family.

definition: Adding liquid to a hot pan after searing protein, then scraping up the browned fond left behind. The fond dissolves into the liquid and forms the foundation of a quick pan sauce.

substitutions.note: drier, more acidic finish; pairs with poultry and pork
rescue option when you have neither wine nor stock; use sparingly

definition: The pre-cook prep step: every ingredient measured, chopped, and within arm's reach before the heat goes on. Reduces cook-time scrambling and is the single biggest source of confidence for new cooks.
origin: Brigade-system kitchen organisation codified by Auguste Escoffier.

definition: Briefly cooking food in boiling water then plunging it into ice water. Sets vivid colour in green vegetables, eases peeling on tomatoes and almonds, and partially cooks ingredients ahead of a quick finish.

substitutions.note: lower nutrient loss; slightly less even colour

definition: The smoky, slightly charred aroma a well-seasoned wok imparts to food cooked over very high heat with constant motion. Hard to reproduce on home stoves; cast-iron pans get partway there.

regionalNames.name: guo qi (the breath of the wok)

substitutions.note: the home-stove proxy; preheat fully and don't crowd
the only true reproduction; >50,000 BTU recommended

definition: A slow-cooked tomato, onion, garlic, and pepper base, often with paprika. Distinct from Italian soffritto, which is tomato-less and more aromatic-vegetable forward.

substitutions.note: deeper, smokier finish; suits chorizo dishes

definition: A fermented soybean paste graded from light (white, sweeter) to dark (red, more savoury and pungent). Whisked into broths, glazes, marinades, and dressings for instant umami depth.

substitutions.note: softer, sweeter; cut quantity by ~25% if a recipe assumes red
rough vegetarian stand-in for white miso in dressings

definition: Both a slow-cooked North African stew and the conical earthenware vessel it cooks in. The cone shape catches steam, condenses it, and returns liquid to the pot for a long, gentle braise.

substitutions.note: good substitute vessel; cooks slightly drier — add a splash of stock

definition: Pasta cooked to a state where it still resists the bite slightly — firm at the centre, not soft through. Drained a minute before package timing and finished in the sauce so it absorbs flavour.
origin: Italian home cooking; literally 'to the tooth'.

definition: Raw fish 'cooked' by the acidity of citrus juice — usually lime — with onion, chili, and coriander. The proteins denature without heat in fifteen to thirty minutes.

substitutions.note: shorter marinade time — they 'cook' in 5-8 minutes

definition: A red chili paste from Tunisia made with roasted peppers, garlic, caraway, and coriander, bound with olive oil. Spread on bread, swirled into stews, or used as a marinade base.

substitutions.note: very different profile — sweeter, fermented — but works as a heat substitute in marinades

substitutions.swap: smoked paprika + cayenne + garlic + olive oil
substitutions.note: rough pantry-staple version; lacks the caraway top-note

definition: A one-pot rice and lentil porridge, gently spiced and easy on the digestion. Considered comfort food and a default sick-day meal across much of India.

substitutions.note: lighter, faster-cooking; reduce simmer time by ~10 minutes
gluten-free swap (rice is already GF); changes texture but works

origin: Ancient subcontinental dish; documented in Sanskrit and Mughal-era texts.
